frameworks.name: sequential sampling
frameworks.one_line: Decision formed by integrating a sequence of stimulus samples over time.

frameworks.name: evidence accumulation
frameworks.one_line: Per-sample weights characterize how evidence is combined into an estimate.

frameworks.name: temporal interruption
frameworks.one_line: A break in the sample stream modifies the temporal weighting profile.

core_question: How does a short temporal break within a sample sequence affect the subjective weighting profile of sensory integration?
key_assumptions: Subjects integrate discrete stimulus samples toward a single estimate
Sample weighting can be recovered via regression on trial-by-trial estimates
Temporal continuity of the sequence is itself an integration-relevant variable
manipulations: Presence vs absence of a 1.75 s break after the 4th of 8 samples
Block design: no-break, break, and randomly interleaved blocks
key_findings: Interrupting the sequence did not significantly change overall estimation performance
No-break condition produced a monotonically decreasing weighting profile (primacy)
Break condition produced a 'double-primacy' profile with a second peak after the break
The double-primacy effect persisted when break and no-break trials were randomly interleaved
interpretations: Temporal regularity of the input shapes how sensory samples are weighted during integration
An interruption appears to reset or restart the integration window rather than degrade overall accuracy
